@svinota entry here can have several types, actually all kind of values that ipset modules can contains. It means:
 * interface names (as string)
 * IP addresses/network (with or without netmask)
 * MAC addresses
 * marks/ports (as int)

And we can mix sometimes both or three of them. We know the real type of each value thanks to "etype" parameter, based on "stype" of ipset (example: one ipset stype="hash:net,iface" will take one entry etype="net,iface").

The code was at beginning only written for ip or networks one single dimensions, so strings was not so curious. When we added several dimensions support, the split/zip made easier (at the beginning only, probably) to understand how to call the function.

In my opinion, it's perfectly fine to use a tuple when there are several values, and avoid cast when not necessary (like "mark" and "port" type). We probably should check "entry" variable type at the beginning and split on comma only when it's a string (and only when etype contains several types)

However, it's begin to be more funky when a type (like port here) can take one or more argument. Entry will be a tuple for two reasons:

 > entry = ("10.10.10.10/32", 10) # entry for net,port ipsets
 > entry = (50, 80) # for a bitmap:port, it's a range of ports
 > entry = ("10.0.0.1/32", (50, 80) # for a bitmap:ip,port ipset (it does not exist today I think, but there are sometimes really curious ipsets)

It will be hard to discriminate between normal tuples (because of several types) and port tuple (or other range tuple, we need a least to support IP ranges). In case of range, I vote for a tuple of tuple, so to change the second example by:

  > entry = ((50, 80),)  # easier to iterate on the list of values. It's of course a range
  > entry = ("192.168.0.1", "192.168.0.254")  # it's for a hash:net,net ipset
  > entry = ((192.168.0.1", "192.168.0.254"), ) # it's for a bitmap:ip ipset (with a range)

So, if it's ok for you, that will be something like:

def _entry_to_data_attrs(self, entry, etype, family):
  attrs = []
  [...}
  types = etype.split(",")
  if len(types) > 1 and isinstance(entry, str):
    entry = entry.split(",")
    assert len(entry) == len(types)
  # back to previous code
  for e, t in zip(entry, types):
    [...]
    if t == "port":
      if isinstance(e, (tuple, list)):
        # do a range
     else:
        # do a single port

I'd vote for making the SBP protocol version a tuple of `u8`s or something else thats easy to compare programmatically and is more efficient to send in frequent message such as the `MSG_HEARTBEAT`.

Standardization would be good eventually but I don't think we should get too deep into that right now? I'd say we should make the best choice for the SBP version and use that going forward and we can improve the other versioning schemes later.

For the FW versions I think strings make more sense anyway as they are primarily just for displaying to the user.

Requesting the description knowing the id using an object:

```js
variable.possibleValues.free_lodger
```

Using an array:

```js
variable.possibleValues.find(function(item) {
  return item.name == 'free_lodger'
})
```

For the user, the array is much more annoying to request. Using an array makes sense only if the order actually _means_ something. It doesn't in our case. 

If you want the legislation explorer to always show the item in the same order:
- Either you're pessimistic, and you implement yourself some kind of alphabetical sorting.
- Either you're optimisitc, and you consider that even though objects don't have an order, browsers and flask tend to serialise and deserialise them in a deterministic way. Which means than _most likely_, the order in which the items will show will stay consistent.

I always like reducing layers of indirection when possible, and I'd love to more clearly define what each layer is for - and if there isn't a good reason, remove it.

Just for additional context we've generally tried to follow this pattern:

* resource layer should basically mirror server state
* vuex state and getters should basically mirror the app-wide UI
* view computed properties should perform component-specific transformations

Very often the visible UI basically does mirror the server state, leading to the tedious mappers. In some situations though, it might not - e.g. if the UI only needs a subset of the API's data (using `fields` would help with this) or requires multiple requests or subsequent transformation.

At one point we'd even considered tying the resource layer more closely to vuex state, and trying to reference server models directly through vuex getters. However it seemed it harder to write code that performed efficient server queries and deal with asynchronous loading logic...

This is intentional.  I spent a decent amount of time trying to figure out a sane way to do this and this is the result.  Here are the properties I was able to achieve.

- the `__call__` method is required to be implemented on subclasses.
- call sites which call this class still do correct type checks on the function signature (i.e. we don't loose type safety when this function is being called)
- all `__call__` implementations on subclasses require a `# type: ignore` comment.
- we don't have to manually instantiate a `Request` object in order to use the API (**this one is important**)

I've added a comment to both of the `Manager` subclasses noting this.

I'm open to alternate solutions but all that I've seen have trade-offs that I'm not ok with (like requiring a `Request` object to use the API.

Well, parts of the `net` module are independent from the peer pool (e.g. version) and by the time that the peer pool module is created, we don't have a peer pool yet. The peer pool is created asynchronously and it takes several seconds until it is set here: https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/f66d296074c0603eefa4e4c718421956d70d183e/p2p/server.py#L262

In other words, when you start trinity, the `net` module is already set up and answers to `net_peerCount` request and does therefore return `0x0` for when the `peer_pool` is still `None`. Which at least technically is correct :)

Because of the rate limit. When running on gLinux machine, it's very likely you'll get 403 from GitHub API because someone has eaten 60 queries per hour per IP that are allowed for anonymous queries.

```sh
$ curl 2>/dev/null https://api.github.com/rate_limit | jq .resources.core
{
  "limit": 60,
  "remaining": 31,
  "reset": 1531350123
}
```

Authenticated users get higher limits. It's not that important so the script is just doing its best trying to locate a config in the default place and does not fail if there is no config, or no token there.

`create_api_call` is not the case because, as is discussed in #104, `create_api_call` is called in the constructor like
```
self._create_shelf = gax.create_api_call(stub.create_shelf, defaults['create_shelf'])
```
and then the call options is passed in the individual method invocation, like:
```
def create_shelf(self, options):
   self._create_shelf(req, options, ...)
```

So the result of `create_api_call` should receive a CallOptions instance, and handling the overrides -- that is the role of merge method.

Some comments on the description of the new `session_id` argument of `Session.__init__()`:

1. On "An opaque string that provides a cryptographically strong identifier of the API session (known as a session id)": I suggest to use the same wording we use for the `session_id` property: "session ID".

2. The description does not make it clear enough that providing a session ID puts the session into the logged-on state.

3. It is not clear how the `session_id` and the `userid` / `password` arguments play together.
 
I suggest the following description for the `session_id` argument, to address these issues:

```
    A session ID returned by the HMC that is to be used for this session.
    Supplying a session ID puts the session into a logged-on state.

    The `userid` and `password` arguments may be omitted if a session ID
    is supplied. If they are omitted, expiration of the session ID causes an exception
    to be raised by subsequent operations, instead of performing an automatic
    re-logon.
```
